bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes escalation risk, a “fragile truce,” and wider regional fallout, framing the strikes as retaliation amid worsening instability; right-leaning outlets foreground Iranian culpability and U.S. force, using sharper language like “blatant,” “regime,” “massive,” and “pay the price” to cast Tehran as the aggressor and American action as justified punishment.
Center-Leaning pieces stay more reportorial, stressing sequence and official claims.

The key dividing lines are Iran’s closure of Hormuz, the report of about 140 targets, and Hegseth’s “bad choice” warning—details both sides share, but interpret through opposing lenses on deterrence, legitimacy, and regional power.
summary: On Sunday, the United States military hit 140 Iranian military targets in its third round of strikes this week, as Iran retaliated with attacks across Oman, Jordan, Qatar, Bahrain, and Kuwait, escalating Middle East tensions.

President Donald Trump threatened Friday to "decimate" Iran if it acts on assassination threats, while Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei pledged "revenge" for his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's killing by the United States and Israel.

Iranian forces "blatantly attacked" a Cyprus-flagged container ship near the Strait of Hormuz, leaving one crew member missing, while separate missile strikes injured three people in Qatar and hit military sites in Jordan.

Mediators are pushing for diplomacy between the United States and Iran, as Oman drafted a tentative proposal Saturday to manage traffic routes in the Strait of Hormuz where nuclear negotiations cannot progress until security is established.

Retired US Air Force Colonel Cedric Leighton characterized the recurring strikes as a "battle rhythm," while Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi held diplomatic calls Sunday amid the volatile standoff between Washington and Tehran.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as another surge in Middle East escalation, stressing U.S. retaliation and broader US-Iran tensions, while right-leaning outlets cast Iran’s move as a hostile “blockade” or “closed until further notice” moment that threatens shipping and security and invites “severe” retaliation.

Both sides spotlight the Strait’s strategic importance and the U.S. strikes that followed, but they diverge on tone: the left leans into conflict-cycle language like “escalates again” and “Now they pay,” while the right uses sharper security rhetoric, including “illegal route” and “Breaking.” Center coverage stays more restrained, often noting a “fragile ceasefire” and reported details without the same partisan heat.
summary: On Sunday, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps closed the Strait of Hormuz until further notice after intercepting a vessel it accused of violating navigation directives.

Escalation followed President Donald Trump declaring the June 17 Islamabad memorandum of understanding "over," prompting Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi to accuse Washington of violating the agreement through new sanctions.

Reuters reported Saturday that the Iranian navy fired a "warning shot" at a vessel that "attempted to transit along an unapproved route," while the IRGC claimed several vessels breached transit rules.

Blaming "illegal foreign intervention" for the incident, the IRGC warned that new military action would trigger a "severe" response, threatening to target additional United States and allied military bases.

The United States administration set a deadline for Iran to confirm the Strait remained open, hours before Trump threatened to "completely decimate" Iran using "1,000 missiles" that were "locked and loaded.
title: Iran declares Strait of Hormuz closed as 'unauthorised' vessel hit

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the subpoenas as intimidation, stressing a “war on press,” “attack on press freedom,” and “should shock the conscience,” while right-leaning coverage reframes the same move as a “leak probe” or “grand jury” investigation tied to “security” and possible unauthorized disclosure.

The key dividing line is whether the reporters are portrayed as targets of authoritarian pressure or as participants in a sensitive national-security story about a Qatari-gifted Air Force One and reported missing antimissile protections.
Center coverage stays more restrained, but both sides agree the subpoenas escalate a serious clash between the administration and the press.
summary: On Friday, the Trump administration issued subpoenas to several New York Times journalists after the paper reported this week on security concerns involving the president's new Qatari-donated Air Force One.

Scrutiny intensified after an abrupt plane swap during a July 2026 NATO summit trip, when the president switched to an older jet amid a collapsing cease-fire with Iran and attacks on Gulf Arab states.

The subpoenas target reporters Julian E. Barnes, Eric Lipton, Tyler Pager, and Eric Schmitt, demanding testimony before a Manhattan grand jury; The White House spokesman Steven Cheung called the aircraft 'state-of-the-art.'
This move mirrors earlier Justice Department efforts to compel testimony from reporters at The Washington Post and The Wall Street Journal, though the DOJ later withdrew those subpoenas.
title: Trump Administration Subpoenas New York Times Reporters Over Air Force One Stories

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Platner’s exit as a Democratic problem-solving moment, stressing how the party can “salvage” the race and manage the replacement process, while right-leaning coverage treats it as a collapse story, leaning on terms like “scandal,” “disgraced,” and “far-left” to portray his departure as a broader ideological failure.

The sharpest dividing line is the exit message: the right spotlights “F**k ICE” and “Free Palestine” as a provocative parting shot, whereas the left de-emphasizes that symbolism and centers the stakes for Senate control.
Both sides agree the withdrawal followed controversy and forces Democrats to act quickly.
summary: On Friday, the Maine Secretary of State's Office confirmed Graham Platner officially withdrew from the Senate race, requesting removal from the ballot following his campaign suspension earlier this week.

Platner, a 41-year-old oyster farmer and retired Marine, faced calls from fellow Democrats to exit the race following a Politico report alleging he sexually assaulted an ex-girlfriend nearly five years ago.
Platner has denied the accusation, saying any claims about "non-consensual behavior" are "categorically untrue," after announcing Wednesday he would suspend his campaign.
Maine Democrats must now find a replacement candidate capable of challenging Republican Senator Susan Collins for the GOP-held seat, with a July 27 deadline per state law.

The Maine Democratic Party will hold a nominating convention featuring roughly 600 delegates to select a successor before the July 27 deadline, with candidates required to gather at least 500 signatures including at least 50 from eight counties.
title: Platner officially withdraws from Maine Senate race

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the dispute as a broad corporate wrongdoing story, stressing words like “stolen,” “scheme,” and “at every level” to portray OpenAI’s gadget push as coordinated misuse of trade secrets, while also spotlighting the earlier Apple -OpenAI partnership as a striking backdrop.
Center-Leaning coverage stays more restrained, adding specifics about “two former employees” and senior leadership without the same dramatic edge.
Right-Leaning coverage de-emphasizes the internal details and instead amplifies scale with “blockbuster,” turning the case into a high-stakes AI hardware clash.
Both sides agree on the lawsuit and trade secret allegations; the divide is between systemic betrayal and sensational industry conflict.
summary: On Friday, Apple filed a lawsuit against OpenAI in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging the artificial intelligence lab engaged in trade secret theft to develop its own consumer hardware.
Relations between the companies chilled after OpenAI purchased IO Products for $6.4 billion last year, marking the lab's direct competition with Apple's consumer electronics business .

OpenAI Chief Hardware Officer Tang Tan allegedly directed job candidates to bring 'actual parts' from Apple to interviews for "'show and tell' sessions in which he and his team at OpenAI can elicit still more Apple confidential information," Apple said in the filing.
Apple is asking the court to bar OpenAI from using or disclosing its trade secrets and require the company to return confidential materials and preserve evidence related to the case.

While ChatGPT remains integrated into Apple Intelligence, Apple is shifting its Siri assistant to Google's Gemini AI models, though the company did not comment on whether the lawsuit affects the ongoing partnership.
title: Apple sues OpenAI, two former employees for trade secrets theft

bias: All three sides agree Lindsey Graham died at 71 after a brief, sudden illness, but they diverge in what his life meant: left-leaning coverage broadens him into a foreign-policy and Israel-focused figure, while right-leaning coverage narrows him to a Trump loyalist, treating “ally/supporter” as the defining lens.

The sharpest divide is interpretive, not medical language: one side highlights Senate influence and national-security stature, the other foregrounds political allegiance and broader ideological stakes.
Tone stays mostly restrained, though phrases like “staunch,” “hawk,” and “Trump supporter” signal distinct priorities, with center coverage adding a softer privacy-and-prayers note.
summary: Senator Lindsey Graham , the veteran South Carolina Republican, died Saturday following a "brief and sudden illness," his office confirmed early Sunday.
Elected to the Senate in 2003, Graham became a leading foreign policy voice and hawkish advocate for Israel, Russia, and Iran, chairing the Senate Budget Committee.
Emergency personnel responded to a call for "cardiac arrest" at Graham's Capitol Hill home Saturday night; NBC News reported photographs showing paramedics removing an individual on a stretcher.
Graham had recently secured the Republican nomination for a fifth Senate term and returned Friday from Kyiv, Ukraine, where he met with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy.
This development follows news that Republican Sen. Mitch McConnell remains in the hospital after paramedics responded to a call at his residence last month, with few details disclosed.
title: Sen. Lindsey Graham dies at 71 after ‘brief and sudden illness’

summary: On Saturday, Unifor and Ford Motor Company reached a tentative three-year national labor contract covering 5,150 union members after extending negotiations beyond a Friday deadline.
Unifor began these negotiations on June 22, selecting Ford as the initial bargaining partner because the automaker demonstrated the most commitment to continuing its Canadian operations.
The Unifor Ford Master Bargaining Committee unanimously endorsed the tentative deal, with ratification meetings scheduled between July 17 and July 19 for workers across Ontario and Alberta.

"We look forward to bringing this tentative agreement to our members," said Unifor Ford Master Bargaining Chair John, as the union seeks to set this pattern for General Motors and Stellantis negotiations.

The broader automotive sector faces instability as 6,000 workers have been laid off across the Big Three; Unifor National President Lana Payne urges federal policy to prioritize domestic manufacturing investments.
title: Ford and Unifor strike tentative deal for three-year labour contract

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the anniversary as a multilateral legal rebuke, stressing words like “reaffirm,” “invalidating,” “illegal,” and “final, binding” to portray China ’s claims as overreach.

Right-Leaning coverage shifts to a sovereignty-and-stability lens, foregrounding Japan’s opposition to “unilaterally change the status quo” and, in some cases, the Philippines’ sovereignty, with less emphasis on the broader coalition’s legal condemnation.
Centered coverage is more restrained, but still highlights the ruling as “final, legally binding” and rejects “destabilizing” actions.
Both sides agree on the 2016 ruling’s importance, yet diverge over whether the story is chiefly about law, power, or regional order.
summary: On Sunday, 14 nations reaffirmed the 2016 South China Sea arbitral ruling on its 10th anniversary, declaring it "final, legally binding, and definitive" while rejecting China's expansive maritime claims.
The 2016 tribunal ruled that China's "nine-dash line" claim lacks legal basis under UNCLOS; Beijing continues to reject the decision, calling it "illegal, null and void."

Australian Ambassador Marc Innes-Brown announced $18 million in new maritime assistance in Manila as foreign envoys urged stronger security cooperation to improve domain awareness and operational capabilities.

Stratbase Institute president Victor Andres "Dindo" Manhit noted that 86% of Filipinos support continued defense of the West Philippine Sea, emphasizing the need for a modern, integrated defense posture.

Ambassadors stressed that defending international law requires practical cooperation, including joint drills and intelligence sharing, to counter "destabilizing" actions threatening regional peace and security in the Indo-Pacific.
title: US, UK and 12 other nations reaffirm 2016 ruling invalidating China’s claims in South China Sea

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the rule as a pro-consumer win, stressing “consumer protection,” “combat,” “crack down,” and “subscription scams,” plus the bigger payoff: up to $162.5 million in annual savings and a link to the mayor’s affordability agenda.
Right-Leaning coverage is slimmer and more procedural, spotlighting the ban and effective date while de-emphasizing consumer gains or ideological framing.

The sharpest dividing line is how each side treats “junk fees” and “subscription traps”: left outlets present them as predatory corporate tactics needing intervention, while the right treats them as the regulatory target itself.
Both agree on the basic policy and its October 1 rollout, reflecting a shared anti-fee baseline.
summary: On Friday, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani announced a "Click to Cancel" rule requiring businesses to make subscription cancellations as easy as sign-ups, effective October 1.
Mamdani signed executive orders in January targeting deceptive subscription practices and "Junk Fees," positioning the rule as central to his affordability agenda aimed at protecting working people.

Businesses must allow cancellation through the same enrollment method, with penalties starting at $525 per violation; the Roosevelt Institute projects annual savings of $162.5 million for New Yorkers.
Enforcement begins October 1, with DCWP Commissioner Samuel Levine stating residents can report violations by calling 311; at least nine states have already enacted similar protections.

The city is also proposing a "Junk Fees" rule requiring transparent upfront pricing, while a prior national "Click to Cancel" rule introduced by Former President Joe Biden's administration was struck down by federal courts.
title: New York City to ban firms from using subscription traps, officials say

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames McGregor’s 69-second return as a “devastating,” “humiliating” collapse, spotlighting disappointment, the crowd’s reaction, and even Drake’s $1 million bet loss, while right-leaning coverage leans into “long-awaited,” “cut short,” and “hype train crashed,” stressing “injury heartbreak,” career doubts, and Dana White’s blown-ACL concern.
Center coverage stays more stripped-down, using live-update/result language and de-emphasizing judgment.

The sharpest dividing line is whether the injury is portrayed mainly as personal failure or as a dramatic comeback setback; all sides, though, agree the bout ended quickly after an apparent leg/knee injury and Holloway won by stoppage.
summary: On Saturday, July 11, 2026, Conor McGregor's return at UFC 329 ended abruptly when he suffered a right knee injury 1:09 into the first round, awarding Max Holloway a TKO victory at Las Vegas' T-Mobile Arena.

The 37-year-old Irishman's first professional bout in five years followed a lengthy recovery from a broken leg sustained during his 2021 match against American Dustin Poirier, which had sidelined his career.
Massive demand for the comeback generated a gate exceeding $25 million with nearly 20,000 fans in attendance; McGregor earned a base salary of $15 million for the main event.
"What can I say," Holloway said, "I had him weak in the knees." The victor expressed interest in a potential rematch once McGregor recovers from the injury.

Legal controversies, including a 2024 civil court finding ordering McGregor to pay $248,000 in damages for sexual assault, have intensified scrutiny on his status as a headliner, complicating his career future.
title: Conor McGregor suffers early injury in return and loses to Max Holloway at UFC 329

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Kennedy Center dispute as a Trump-driven “scheme,” leaning on charged terms like “bombshell,” “no-bid contracts,” and “flunkies” to portray cronyism, rushed renovations, and ignored contracting rules as part of a broader pattern of corruption.

Center-Leaning coverage is more procedural, emphasizing a Senate “probe,” “alleged corruption,” and “mismanaged” funds, with “seize control” and “travails” signaling concern but less personal indictment.
The key dividing line is whether the story is about Trump’s political meddling or institutional oversight.
Both sides still converge on whistleblower allegations, rushed work, and Kennedy Center turmoil, reflecting deeper splits over power, accountability, and elite influence.
summary: On Saturday, Senator Sheldon Whitehouse of Rhode Island released a whistleblower disclosure from the Government Accountability Project alleging the Kennedy Center rushed renovations driven by President Donald Trump 's aesthetic preferences and desire to host televised events.
Trump seized control of the Kennedy Center at the start of his second term, replacing prior leadership with a Board of Trustees that named him chairman and initiated rapid renovations.

Whistleblowers detailed an $8 million no-bid flooring contract awarded to Low Country Flooring and a bathroom floor redone because the president disliked the beige tile color, while steel columns are reportedly rusting through fresh paint.

Whitehouse demanded answers from Kennedy Center executive director Matt Floca by July 23, raising what he called 'serious questions' about whether public funds were being spent properly on contracting practices.

The complaint highlights broader concerns about Trump administration contracting practices, as the Kennedy Center adopted a policy allowing sole-source contracts when 'circumstances beyond the center's control require an immediate award.
title: Democrat announces whistleblower allegations of construction problems at Kennedy Center

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ratings changes as Democratic momentum, spotlighting four races moving toward Dems and even citing Arizona’s fundraising edge and red-state significance; right-leaning coverage uses more cautionary, horse-race language, stressing Ohio’s move from “lean Republican” to “toss up,” Ramaswamy’s “chances of losing,” and “surprisingly competitive” red-leaning states.

The rhetoric diverges too: the left leans into upbeat “gains momentum” framing, while the right favors sharper vulnerability cues like “toss up” and “losing.” Center coverage is more stripped-down and descriptive.
Both sides agree the key dividing line is a broader national climate making Democrats competitive in places usually seen as Republican territory.
summary: On Friday, July 10, 2026, the Cook Political Report shifted ratings in five gubernatorial contests, moving four toward Democrats and potentially positioning the party to hold more governorships nationally than at any time since 2010.

The Cook Political Report reclassified Ohio from Lean Republican to a Toss Up, citing Republican Vivek Ramaswamy 's "clear likeability problem … even some in his own party" as a statistical tie emerged between Democrat Amy Acton and him.

Arizona moved toward Democrats, where incumbent Katie Hobbs likely faces Rep. Andy Biggs, while Maine and New Mexico shifted to Solid Democrat ratings with Democrat Hannah Pingree holding a solid lead over Republican Bobby Charles in Maine.

In Oregon, the report downgraded the governor's race from Solid to Likely Democrat, reflecting incumbent Gov. Tina Kotek's poor approval ratings as she faces state Sen. Christine Drazan in the general election.

Analyst Jessica Taylor noted the shifts reflect a favorable national climate and strong Democratic recruitment, positioning Democrats to reach their highest number of governorships nationally since 2010 if they prevail in these contests.
title: Cook Political Report moving 4 of 5 gubernatorial races toward Democrats

bias: Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, while the right frames Graham as a near-symbolic ally: a “true friend,” “beloved by all,” and tied to “unwavering support” for Israel, with the added dividing line of “anti-Iranian” signaling a hardline foreign-policy lens.
The center is more restrained, using a tribute like “one of Israel’s greatest friends” and noting the death was announced by his office on social media.

Across both, the shared emphasis is Israeli condolences and Graham’s pro-Israel role, but the right intensifies emotional reverence and ideological signaling, turning a memorial into a broader statement about alliance and regional politics .
summary: US Senator Lindsey Graham, a longtime supporter of Israel, died suddenly at age 71 after a brief illness.
Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz and President Isaac Herzog expressed deep sorrow and praised Graham's strong support for Israel's security and US-Israel relations.
Israeli leaders across political lines called Graham a true friend of Israel and highlighted his unwavering commitment to Israel’s security and the US-Israel relationship.
Foreign Minister Gideon Sa'ar and opposition leader Yair Lapid emphasized Graham's steadfast commitment to Israel and peace in the Middle East .
title: Israeli officials offer their condolences after death of US Senator Lindsey Graham

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds the downside, repeatedly framing the deal as “raising concerns” over energy use and environmental impacts, while the right strips the story down to a more compact business -development frame around agreements that “pave the way” and “begin groundwork.” The sharpest dividing line is environmental risk versus economic opportunity: left and center mention the memorandums and named firms, treating them as a concrete but potentially costly step, whereas the right de-emphasized those specifics and stayed more terse.

Tone also diverges: the left leans cautionary, the right more restrained, yet both still converge on the same core fact—Caribbean data centers backed by U.S. companies—reflecting broader tensions over growth, power demand, and environmental tradeoffs.
summary: On Friday, Trinidad and Tobago Prime Minister Kamla Persad-Bissessar signed memorandums with Florida-headquartered Hummingbird AI Holdings and New York-based Ernst and Young LLP to develop large-scale data centers and rejuvenate local industry.
The Government facilitated these agreements, which represent the first such data center partnerships with a Caribbean country, as officials expect the three initiatives to generate over 5,000 jobs.

Hummingbird Holdings proposed a 150 MW AI infrastructure facility, while Ernst and Young LLP plans to partner on a 300 MW data center; Pinnacle Steel and Vanadium Corporation targets steel-plant redevelopment.

Renowned social activist Dr. Wayne Kublalsingh told The Associated Press he is concerned about energy consumption, arguing the Government is "trying to present something which looks like development, but which is not development."

Data centers could account for nearly 3% of global electricity use by 2030, raising concerns that large, water-intensive facilities will strain Trinidad and Tobago's chronic water shortages and existing grid capacity.
title: Trinidad and Tobago signs agreements with US companies that pave the way for data centers

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as accountability, stressing that Byrne’s claims were “lies” and tying him to an “ally of Donald Trump,” while right-leaning outlets turn the same $1.7 million award into a partisan spectacle, using phrases like “BREAKING,” “stunning,” “falls a** backwards,” and “seven-figure day” to mock Hunter Biden or spotlight drama.

The key dividing line is whether the judgment is presented as a substantive win or a procedural/default twist, with the right also foregrounding the “$800M Iran Bribery Scheme” allegation and Hunter’s biography.
All sides agree on the court award, but they split over its meaning amid broader Biden-Trump political fault lines.
summary: On Friday, U.S. District Judge Stephen Wilson awarded Hunter Biden $1.7 million in punitive damages plus $1 in nominal damages and about $35,000 in court sanctions against Patrick Byrne, the former CEO of Overstock.com, in a defamation case.
Biden sued Byrne in 2023, alleging the defendant falsely claimed in an interview that Biden sought an $800 million bribe from Iran to influence U.S. policy while President Joe Biden was in office.
Judge Wilson wrote that Byrne failed to provide evidence supporting the claims and found "ample evidence" that Byrne knew the story was false and engaged in intentional misrepresentation.
The court entered default against Byrne after he failed to appear and repeatedly disobeyed orders, while continuing to promote statements linking the plaintiff to the October 7 attacks by Hamas.

Byrne, a vocal Donald Trump supporter, resigned as Overstock CEO in 2019 after disclosing a romantic relationship with convicted Russian spy Maria Butina and has promoted conspiracy theories about Covid vaccines.
title: Judge awards Hunter Biden $1.7M in defamation suit against ex-Overstock CEO

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as the legal cleanup of Jan. 6 fallout, stressing “remaining” convictions, “rioting Proud Boys ,” and even the judge’s “disgust,” while tying it to Trump’s “sweeping pardons” and broader accountability.

Right-Leaning coverage recasts the same event as the judge “siding with DOJ,” foregrounding “seditious conspiracy convictions” and the “most serious criminal judgments” to emphasize legal gravity and procedural vindication.
The key dividing lines are Jan. 6 itself, the Proud Boys’ ideological label, and Trump’s pardons.
Both sides agree the judge dismissed the case, but they split over whether that signals accountability, relief, or a politically charged reversal.
summary: On Friday, U.S. District Judge Timothy Kelly dismissed the convictions of four Proud Boys members for their actions during the Jan. 6, 2021, Capitol riot, closing the case permanently with prejudice.
President Donald Trump 's Executive Order signed on his first day in office mandated the dismissal, directing the Attorney General to pursue closure of these Jan. 6 defendants' cases.

While granting the motion, Kelly wrote that the court "lacks the authority to compel the Executive to pursue a prosecution," emphasizing the ruling should not be mistaken for his agreement with the decision.

Previously convicted of seditious conspiracy, defendants Ethan Nordean, Joseph Biggs, Zachary Rehl, and Dominic Pezzola saw their case end, with Rehl celebrating on social media that it was "ALL OVER!".

These dismissals reflect a broader Trump administration policy toward Jan. 6 defendants, as Trump touted an "anti-weaponization fund" to compensate those he claims were unfairly targeted, though a judge recently blocked it.
title: Judge tosses remnants of Proud Boys seditious conspiracy case after Trump’s broad clemency

bias: Left and right coverage largely converge on the same emergency, but they differ in texture: left headlines lean on urgency with terms like “fast-spreading” and “rapidly growing,” while right framing adds distance with “sparsely populated,” subtly softening human impact even as it still stresses the blaze’s speed.

The key dividing line is emphasis—left highlights the remote high-desert setting near Llano/Antelope Valley, while right centers evacuation orders and scale, including the 2,200-acre figure, as a serious local threat.
Center coverage sits between them, adding heat-wave context and detailed timing, and all sides share a basic focus on dry, scorching conditions and immediate public-safety risk.
summary: A fast-spreading brush fire ignited in Los Angeles County on Friday afternoon, forcing evacuation orders in a sparsely populated area and reaching 2,200 acres by evening.
Dry and scorching conditions fueled the fire in a remote high desert region about 45 miles northeast of Los Angeles, where temperatures soared to nearly 100 degrees Fahrenheit.
Angeles National Forest officials reported that parts of a census-designated community of about 7,200 people in San Bernardino County were under an evacuation warning by Friday evening.
Officials provided updates on the blaze through social media, though it remains unclear how many buildings are threatened by the fire according to the County Fire Department.
A strong, long-lasting heat dome is driving a heat wave across many regions this weekend, adding to an already high wildfire risk this year in many drought-stricken regions.
title: Rapidly growing wildfire in Los Angeles County prompts evacuations in remote high desert

bias: Left-Leaning coverage leans into the pre-crash mystery, spotlighting an “unusual vibration” and the NTSB report as the key explanation, while center and right coverage shift toward emergency-management context, stressing pilots searching for “flat areas” and controllers saying there were “none nearby.” The shared “flamed out” phrasing makes the failure sound abrupt, but the left frames it as a mechanical warning sign and the others as a sudden crisis with limited options.
The main divide is interpretive: warning indicator versus emergency response.
All sides agree on the crash, death, injuries, and engine failure, reflecting a common focus on aviation safety and official investigation.
summary: On Friday, the National Transportation Safety Board released a preliminary report revealing that dual engine failure forced a business jet to crash on a Texas highway on June 16, preventing pilots from reaching Laredo International Airport.

Flight crew members reported an "unusual vibration" early in the flight, later communicating "multiple other failures" and "fuel level low" to Houston air traffic controllers as they attempted to divert to Laredo.

The crew "maneuvered the airplane to touch down" on the highway about 1.6 kilometers from the airport, shearing light poles and striking a vehicle. The crash killed Austin tech entrepreneur Joshua Baer.

Five people escaped the burning wreckage after the main cabin door was opened. NetJets, the company operating The Cessna Citation Latitude, said, "Our thoughts remain with everyone affected by this heartbreaking tragedy."

The NTSB preliminary report does not contain a "probable cause" for the crash. NTSB investigator Mitchell Gallo stated a final report will be released in approximately one year once the investigation is completed.
title: Both engines flamed out before small jet crashed in June on Texas highway, NTSB report says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage centers the teen’s death in custody as a brutal institutional failure, amplifying the coroner’s account with stark terms like “stomped to death,” while right-leaning coverage more strongly keeps the arrest context in view, using phrases like “in connection with a killing” to frame Butler through alleged criminal involvement.
The emotional split is clear: the left leans into outrage and vulnerability, the right into danger and public-safety tension.
Center coverage stays narrower, adding administrative leave and the $1 million bond.
All sides share the coroner’s shoe-print detail, but diverge on whether the story is about abuse, crime, or accountability.
summary: Hinds County Coroner Jeremiah Howard confirmed Thursday that 18-year-old Mielun Butler was stomped to death inside the Hinds County Detention Center in Raymond last week, with shoe prints visible on his head.
Butler had been held on a $1 million bond following his July 2 arrest for the murder of Melvin Edwards at the Pine Ridge Garden Apartments, charged as the second suspect in the case.
Sheriff Tyree Jones confirmed Monday that a viral social media video showing Butler beaten and lying face down on concrete is authentic; an officer has been placed on administrative leave with pay.

Jones stated the Mississippi Bureau is conducting a criminal investigation while his office handles administrative review; he declined to name the officer or confirm if they provided the recording device.

Federal judge Carlton Reeves will hold a hearing Friday to discuss jail conditions, while a Hinds County Chancery Court judge ordered the sheriff's office to release death records withheld for over a year.
title: Teen stomped to death in Mississippi jail, coroner says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, so the split is mainly between center and right: both frame Erika Kirk watching surveillance video at her request and preserve the caution of “alleged killer,” but the right repeats “widow,” “requested,” and “husband’s alleged killer” more insistently, turning the event into a grief-and-accountability story.
The center stays more restrained and contextual, noting prosecutors and a broader procedural setup.
Pivotal dividing lines are the widow’s request, the accused man’s status, and Charlie Kirk ’s identity as a “right-wing activist,” which both sides share but use to anchor different emotional tones.
summary: Erika Kirk was allowed to watch surveillance video of Tyler Robinson, accused of killing her husband, Charlie Kirk , after she requested it.
Most of the five-day hearing regarding Tyler Robinson's potential trial was livestreamed, but the surveillance video was shown only in court, not to the public.
Judge Tony Graf balanced transparency with the need for juror impartiality by viewing the video himself and limiting its public disclosure.
A ruling on whether Tyler Robinson should stand trial is expected after arguments scheduled for September 1.
title: Charlie Kirk's widow views video she requested of husband's alleged killer

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Meta ’s Muse Image rollback as a humiliating corporate failure, using sharp language like “didn’t survive the week,” “scrapped already,” “complete U-turn,” and even “AI slop factory” to spotlight backlash, privacy harm, and consent violations; right-leaning coverage is more likely to stress the same privacy concerns as a legitimate check on Big Tech, while also widening the lens to deepfake detection and watermarking, with phrases like “missed the mark” and “storm of criticism.” The key dividing line is whether the story is about public outrage and reputational damage or broader technical risk, though both sides agree the feature’s use of public Instagram content triggered the reversal.
summary: Meta withdrew the Instagram account reference feature on Friday following widespread user backlash. The tool, part of the new Muse Image model, allowed users to create AI-generated images by referencing public profiles.

Launched Tuesday, July 7, the Muse Image model automatically opted in all public profiles, excluding users under 18, sparking immediate uproar. Critics labeled the automatic integration a "privacy landmine waiting to detonate."

The Actors union, SAG-AFTRA, posted a statement on Thursday, July 9, calling the lack of clear opt-in "an utter miscalculation of public sentiment." Agencies including the Creative Artists Agency also raised concerns over potential image repurposing.

Confirming the removal on Friday, Meta stated, "We've heard the feedback that this feature missed the mark, so it's no longer available." The company noted its intent was providing a creative tool with user control.

While the reference feature is discontinued, the broader Muse Image model remains active across Meta's ecosystem. The company continues AI expansion, with Muse Video expected to arrive soon despite ongoing scrutiny of generative technology.
title: Meta AI image detector fails to identify some of its own cropped AI images, Reuters analysis finds

bias: Both perspectives frame the SEC’s rule as a jolt to hedge funds, using the shared language of “rattle” and “keep secret” to cast activist investors as under pressure and secrecy as the central tension.

The main split is emphasis: the right-leaning coverage adds the specific hook of “13D filings,” sharpening the regulatory mechanism, while the center version stays tightly on the disclosure requirement itself.

Neither side turns the story into a culture-war battle; instead, they converge on transparency versus concealment, with the emotional undertone suggesting disruption for powerful financiers rather than celebration or alarm.
summary: The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission requires activist investors to disclose their clients' identities in regulatory filings, challenging hedge funds that prefer confidentiality.
The SEC issued updated guidance on 13D filings and proxy statements that lawyers handling activist investor matters found unexpected and not widely publicized.
This new interpretation clarifies SEC rules on key disclosures following recent active campaigns by activist investors targeting various companies.
The SEC explicitly states that investors forming entities to acquire securities for activism must reveal their identities, as referenced in their response to Question 110.09.
title: US activist investors must disclose clients in filings, SEC says
topic_full: Securities and Exchange Commission · United States

bias: Left-Leaning coverage turns the Walter Reed update into a health alarm, using words like “fears,” “admits,” “baffling,” and “torpedoes” to suggest Trump’s phrasing backfires and deepens worries about age and cognition; it also stresses the unusual repeat visits.
Right-Leaning coverage is comparatively calmer but still pivots on Trump’s self-presentation, highlighting “perfect,” “aced,” and his claim of routine six-month checkups as a rebuttal to scrutiny.
The key dividing line is whether the story is a warning sign or a self-defense.
Both sides center his “just finished” exam and cognitive test claims, reflecting broader tensions over presidential fitness and trust.
summary: On Saturday, President Donald Trump claimed on Truth Social he 'just' completed a 'perfect physical' at Walter Reed Medical Center; a White House official later told CNN the statement referred to his May 26 examination.

Trump's post followed a week of public gaffes, including misidentifying Iran as the 'Islamic State of Japan' and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy as Vladimir Putin, while attacking journalist Maggie Haberman.
Contradicting the claim, the president was golfing on Saturday when he posted, and no reports indicate an exam occurred after his May 26 physical, raising questions about the timing.

The president's use of 'just' prompted confusion among journalists like Olivier Knox, who questioned the exam's timing, as critics argue the administration's health reporting is a 'black box' releasing limited information.

Observers remain skeptical of official health disclosures, particularly after previous claims that doctors declared he would live for 200 years during his May visit, reflecting ongoing transparency concerns.
title: Trump says he 'just finished' a physical at Walter Reed

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as states “block” harmful “funding cuts” to school mental health services, stressing public protection and the 2018 school-shooting origin of the grants; right-leaning coverage sharpens the legal clash, highlighting words like “illegal,” “unlawfully,” and “circumvent” to portray a court-order fight over “$1B” and “termination” of congressionally approved funds.

The center sits between them, adding the more political note about “Democrat-controlled states” and “political reasons.” Both sides agree the lawsuit is about school mental health grants, but they diverge on whether the key story is safeguarding students or condemning federal overreach.
summary: On Friday, a coalition of 15 states sued the administration of President Donald Trump to block the planned termination of federal school-based mental health grants scheduled for the end of this month.

Congress allocated $1 billion in 2022 for the Mental Health Service Professional Demonstration Grant Program following the Uvalde, Texas school shooting, backed by Republican U.S. Senators John Cornyn, Susan Collins, and Thom Tillis.

Trump administration officials claimed the grants conflicted with administration priorities, defying a court order from last July that protected the program serving nearly 775,000 students nationwide.

Massachusetts Attorney General Andrea Joy Campbell stated the administration lacks power to arbitrarily revoke services; the new filing addresses gaps in the prior court order that could permit funding termination.

Rhode Island Attorney General Peter F. Neronha emphasized that grants help students cope with loneliness and violence; the Department of Education did not respond to requests for comment on the termination plan.
title: 15 states sue Trump administration to block school mental health funding cuts
Iran Strikes Gulf Neighbours After New US Attacks

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts the operation as an active pursuit, using words like “hunts,” “trapped,” and “terrorists” to stress danger and ongoing combat, while right-leaning coverage frames it as decisive security action, leaning on phrases like “destroys,” “terror tunnels,” and “fortified” to spotlight Hezbollah entrenchment and threat.
The right also adds technical details—tunnel length, depth, mortars, RPGs, living quarters—to magnify military sophistication; the left instead emphasizes launch shafts and concealed attackers.

A key dividing line is whether the story is a battlefield sweep or evidence of broader hostility, though both sides converge on the IDF uncovering and destroying Hezbollah infrastructure in southern Lebanon.
summary: On Thursday, the Israel Defense Forces dismantled two underground tunnel routes in southern Lebanon near Majdal Zoun, describing the area as a heavily fortified Hezbollah stronghold built to support attacks on Israel.

Hezbollah fortified the village, believing Israeli forces would not advance 12 kilometers into Lebanese territory; the infrastructure included command centers and defensive tunnels designed to control access routes and attack advancing troops.

Soldiers from the 551st Paratroopers Brigade and the Yahalom combat engineering unit uncovered tunnels stretching approximately 200 meters and reaching about 20 meters deep, containing living quarters, weapons, and three rocket launch shafts aimed toward Israeli territory.

Troops increasingly encounter what officials describe as "trapped terrorists"—small groups of Hezbollah fighters isolated in captured areas. One security official said, "This was a terrorist base where weapons are found in every second or third house," alongside Kornet anti-tank missiles.

Search operations continue across Lebanon, with similar encounters reported near Bint Jbeil earlier this week; the military confirmed all located facilities and weapons caches, including mortars and RPGs, have been destroyed to prevent future attacks.
title: Trump Says US Willing to Talk with Iran but Ceasefire over, IDF Uncovers Hezbollah Arms Caches

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Abu Safiya as a humanitarian victim of deliberate, possibly lethal mistreatment, using charged terms like “imminent danger,” “deliberate,” and “scandalous,” and often broadening the story into attacks on doctors, hospitals, and calls for sanctions.
Right-Leaning coverage pivots toward security suspicion, with phrases like “Hamas colonel” and hints of “systematic targeting” or militant imagery, treating the case through a counterterror lens.
Center coverage stays more restrained, stressing “arbitrary” detention and UN/legal findings.
All sides agree he is detained and that UN bodies want his release, but ideology divides the interpretation of why.
summary: On Wednesday, a United Nations inquiry labeled the 18-month detention of Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya, director of Gaza's Kamal Adwan Hospital, arbitrary and urged his immediate release, citing violations of international human rights instruments.

Israeli forces seized Abu Safiya in December 2024, alleging the pediatrician was a Hamas member, though rights groups note authorities have produced no public evidence supporting these combatant claims during his detention without charge.

Lawyer Nasser Odeh reported after a July 2 prison visit that the doctor is nearly unrecognizable, suffering severe injuries to his 'head, eyes, ears, and neck' while struggling to breathe in the Rakefet interrogation facility.
Multiple UK Members of Parliament, including Jeremy Corbyn, former Labour leader, and Nadia Whittome, Labour MP, have urged the government to use diplomatic leverage to secure Abu Safiya's release.

The UN inquiry warned this case 'may indicate a widespread or systematic practice' of arbitrary detention, while UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese criticized the BBC for presenting unproven Israeli military claims regarding the doctor.
title: UN inquiry urges release of Gaza doctor held by Israel since December 2024
Multiple explosions reported across southern Iran as US launches new strikes

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the map as a selective act of recognition, stressing that newer enclaves like “Little Palestine” were included while older neighborhoods were “erased,” with “sparks fury” signaling backlash over cultural omission.

Right-Leaning coverage turns the same dispute into a grievance-driven clash, using phrases like “caught lying,” “fiasco,” and “caves” to portray Mamdani as deceptive and pressured, while elevating Italian-American outrage and the broader “immigrant American dream.” The dividing line is whether the story is about multicultural naming choices or an affront to heritage and assimilation.
Both sides agree Little Italy was left off, but they interpret that omission through sharply different cultural and political lenses.
summary: Mayor Zohran Mamdani 's City Hall published an official map of 30 immigrant enclaves for World Cup tourists, but the initiative sparked 'Mapgate' backlash after omitting historic Italian, Irish, and Jewish neighborhoods.

The Mamdani administration expanded a 2023 list from former Mayor Eric Adams, adding four enclaves; when staff suggested including others, speechwriter Julian Gerson dismissed concerns, noting, "Meh, we can't include everyone."

Italian Americans, along with Irish residents in Woodlawn and Breezy Point, criticized the omissions, while the City Council's Italian caucus, composed of Republicans, accused the Democratic administration of erasing their history.

On Friday night, Mamdani attempted to apologize, stating it was "clear" the map was not an exhaustive list of the city's more than 200 ethnic communities, though the response did little to calm the uproar.

The Mapgate affair joins another controversy involving speechwriter Julian Gerson, who previously omitted Catholics from a speech about persecuted groups, as critics argue the administration intentionally selects which immigrant stories to celebrate and which to sideline.
title: Italian-Americans Blast Mamdani After Excluded from Mayor's Map of NYC Immigrant Neighborhoods

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a culture-war indictment: it spotlights Rasner’s “ MAGA Republican” identity, his “Trump supporter” ties, and portrays GOP figures as “virulently anti-gay,” using words like “whines” and “shocked” to cast the dispute as hypocrisy and prejudice.
Right-Leaning coverage narrows the focus to a defamation fight, stressing “alleged” claims and the “pedophile” slur while softening broader ideological conflict.
The key dividing line is whether the case signals GOP homophobia or simply a legal dispute.
Both sides agree on the basic facts, but their framing tracks deeper partisan divides over identity, speech, and intra-party conflict.
summary: Wyoming GOP congressional candidate Reid Rasner is pursuing defamation lawsuits against fellow party members, alleging a 'whisper campaign' falsely labeled him a 'pedophile' to undermine his campaign.
Former Wyoming GOP state Senator Austin 'Kit' Jennings allegedly orchestrated the effort, which Rasner claims uses homophobic slurs like 'pedophile' and 'groomer' as political weapons.
Wyoming Secretary of State Chuck Gray, a primary rival, drew attention to Rasner's sexuality in a May poll, while the Wyoming Family Alliance barred the candidate from a primary debate.
On Friday, Rasner was expected to settle a defamation case against an Iowa resident who repeatedly shared the false 'pedophile' accusation under his campaign's social media posts.
Despite investing $1.2 million of his own money, Rasner expressed regret he 'should have filed' lawsuits on 'Day One' to combat the hate and threats against his sexuality.
title: Reid Rasner Sues GOP Figures Over Pedophilia Rumors in Wyoming Race

summary: Museum Boijmans Van Beuningen in Rotterdam, Netherlands, reopens the "Pindakaasvloer" to the public Friday for a two-month show, honoring late Dutch artist Wim Schippers.
Schippers, who died last month, first created this work in 1969 as part of his "Floor Covering Series" and voiced Ernie and Kermit the Frog in the Dutch version of "Sesame Street."
Two employees spent last week spreading 40 buckets of peanut butter across a 25-square-meter hexagon, using drywall trowels to apply the mixture to a 2-centimeter thickness.

The installation utilizes more than 800 pounds of peanut butter—enough for around 15,000 sandwiches—and Calvé donated supplies for the 270-square-foot display, previously shown at the Central Museum in Utrecht.

In 1997, visitors "vandalized" the work by placing bread and chocolate sprinkles on the floor; the piece remains remembered for its pungent scent and absurdist nature challenging conventional art ideas.
title: Dutch Museum Covers Floor in Peanut Butter to Honor Late Artist

summary: The Weather Prediction Center forecasts widespread Major to Extreme HeatRisk across the Intermountain West and Plains through July 15, with temperatures exceeding 100°F and isolated highs reaching 109°F.

A strong heat dome formed by high pressure trapping hot air and blocking cooling winds is driving the event; AccuWeather meteorologist Chad Merrill notes it is one of the strongest systems to affect the Dakotas in 25 years.
In Bismarck, North Dakota, temperatures will surpass 100°F until Tuesday, while Las Vegas is expected to hit 111°F on Saturday. Overnight lows will remain around 81°F, offering minimal relief.

Officials advise residents to limit strenuous outdoor activity and stay hydrated, warning the heat could spike fire risk in dry areas including the Rockies. They urge against leaving children or pets unattended in vehicles.

Climate change is causing more intense heat waves, scientists say, while the current El Nino has an 81% chance of becoming "very strong" by fall. The National Weather Service predicts more than 90 local temperature records will be tied or broken.
title: Dangerous heat wave is building, will bring oppressive temperatures to much of the United States

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as an inflation -and-housing squeeze, stressing officials are “divided,” home prices “hit an all-time high,” and even tying outlook shifts to the Iran war, while center coverage broadens that into everyday pain through grocery and gas costs.

Right-Leaning coverage pivots away from consumer strain and instead spotlights institutional skepticism, using phrases like “more divided” and the contrast with a “12–0” vote to suggest hidden discord inside the Fed.
The shared thread is concern over inflation and Fed uncertainty, but the divide reflects broader splits over whether to emphasize household hardship or elite internal fracture.
summary: The Federal Reserve 's rate-setting committee is split over whether inflation will remain elevated or cool once the Iran war ends, according to minutes released this week under new Chair Kevin Warsh.
While existing home sales fell 2.4% last month to a 4.09 million annual rate, median U.S. home prices climbed to an all-time high in June, according to the National Association of Realtors.
Labor Department data shows U.S. jobless claims dipped by 2,000 to 215,000 for the week ending July 4, though employers added only 57,000 jobs in June, signaling business caution.
The International Monetary Fund downgraded its 2026 global economic growth outlook to 3%, citing energy shocks from the Iran war, with growth expected to rebound to 3.4% next year.

Global oil demand may decline by 1 million barrels per day in 2026 due to supply disruptions, the International Energy Agency reports, as Wall Street markets drifted Friday amid uncertainty over the war.
title: Fed officials divided on US inflation views; US home prices hit all-time high

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a fragile diplomatic crisis, stressing “emboldened” Iran, “tests Trump,” and a “crumbling deal,” with added focus on Hormuz shipping risks and possible deadlines; right-leaning coverage is more hawkish, spotlighting “trade threats,” “falls apart,” and “strain” to portray negotiations as collapse under pressure, while also de-emphasizing diplomatic nuance and elevating resistance rhetoric like “global arrogance.” The center stays more restrained, using terms like “renewed fighting” and keeping the mediation angle in view.
Both sides agree the ceasefire is under strain and Hormuz is pivotal, but they diverge over whether the moment signals caution or failure.
summary: U.S. President Donald Trump threatened missile attacks on Iran after mourners called for his assassination at Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei 's funeral.
Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei vowed to avenge his father's death amid escalating tensions in the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran insists on controlling the Strait of Hormuz and charging fees for ships, while the United States demands public guarantees of free passage without tolls.
Diplomatic talks involving Oman and Qatar continue to seek safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz despite rising hostilities.
title: Trump and Iran’s supreme leader trade threats as mediators try to save their crumbling deal

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a potentially bigger, undercounted public- health threat, stressing phrases like “largely undetected” and “true scale,” while right-leaning coverage centers the confirmed “American” case and pairs it with severity markers like the 1,830 cases and 648 deaths, making the outbreak feel immediate but more contained in narrative.
The left leans on WHO warning language; the right uses plainer, more national identity cues and “infected” to sharpen concern.

Center coverage sits between them, emphasizing CDC coordination and “prevent further transmission.” All sides agree on the Congo case and CDC involvement, but diverge on whether the key story is hidden spread or confirmed risk.
summary: On Friday, the CDC reported that a United States citizen working for a humanitarian group in the DRC tested positive for the Bundibugyo strain of the Ebola virus.
Government data released late Friday shows the confirmed Ebola outbreak in the DRC has grown to 1,830 cases, including 648 deaths, amid ongoing community transmission.

No Ebola cases have been confirmed in the United States, and the CDC states "the overall risk to the American public and travelers remains low," with disease limited to remote areas of the DRC and Uganda.

Last month, a humanitarian worker tested positive in France after returning from the DRC, while in May the international charity Serge reported American Christian missionary physician Peter Stafford also "tested positive" for the virus.

Peter Stafford, evacuated to Berlin, Germany for treatment, later described his illness as difficult, while his wife Rebekah Stafford recalled explaining the diagnosis to their four children, all under seven years old.
title: CDC Says U.S. Humanitarian Worker in Congo Tests Positive for Ebola
